does: This paper measures the performance gap between LLM personalization systems evaluated on synthetic data versus real human data. The authors collect 550 human conversations with 19,969 human judgments across three stages: extracting user attributes from conversations, selecting relevant attributes for new prompts, and generating personalized responses. They find that current models significantly underperform on human data compared to synthetic benchmarks, struggling particularly with attribute extraction and relevance selection. When humans evaluate personalized responses, they rate them no better than generic alternatives, despite automated metrics suggesting improvements. The paper proposes two lightweight training-based interventions that partially align automated evaluation with human judgments in the first two stages.
connects: This work builds on growing research in LLM personalization and user adaptation, which has increasingly relied on synthetic evaluation frameworks. The paper directly addresses concerns raised in the broader literature about the reliability of synthetic evaluations for user-facing systems, similar to critiques in human-in-the-loop ML and preference learning. It extends attribute-based personalization approaches by empirically studying the entire pipeline from user modeling through response generation. The work connects to research on instruction-following, in-context learning, and user preference alignment, while responding to the gap between published benchmark results and real-world system performance that has motivated recent work emphasizing human evaluation and grounding of NLP systems.
enables: This paper points toward more rigorous evaluation frameworks for personalization that incorporate human feedback throughout model development. It suggests directions for improving attribute extraction from unstructured conversations, possibly through better prompting strategies or fine-tuning approaches. The identified disagreement between human and model judgments on attribute relevance could motivate research into learning human-aligned preference functions for personalization. The lightweight training interventions demonstrated here could be developed into more sophisticated alignment techniques. More broadly, this work emphasizes the importance of human-centric evaluation in LLM applications and could inspire similar grounded evaluations of other personalization or adaptation systems, potentially driving development of methods that generalize better from synthetic to real user interactions.
